summary: Dan Shapiro shares a woodworking tip about using mineral oil for wet sanding, which produces smoother finishes and dramatically extends sandpaper life. The post is structured as a humorous how-to guide, with the central comedic bit being the awkwardness of buying mineral oil (sold as a laxative) at the grocery store. He explains the technique: apply mineral oil liberally to the surface, then sand with 320 grit for results far superior to dry sanding. The post also touches on his broader sanding workflow, including pore-filling with pumice.
key_points: Mineral oil wet sanding produces smoother surfaces than dry sanding and makes sandpaper last dramatically longer
The cheapest mineral oil comes from the pharmacy/grocery aisle, where it's sold as a laxative — cheaper than the industrial woodworking version
The technique works best as a final step (320 grit) but can be used from the start if you're not filling pores with pumice
Apply enough mineral oil that your hand 'skates' along the surface before sanding
money_quotes: I haven't shared the ultimate secret to fabulous finishes, not to mention saving a bundle on sandpaper: mineral oil wet sanding.
If you happen to have the simultaneous personality defects of being easily embarrased and constantly feeling like you have to explain yourself to total strangers, this may be a bit tricky.

It's funny how it's cheaper to buy the medicinal stuff than it is to buy the industrial stuff. You'd think that if you were actually going to swallow it as a laxitive, they'd have to charge more for it.
I mean the stuff is really cheap. Too bad I'm not constipated. I'm not, you know. Totally regular. Every morning, like clockwork.
Not only with the surface be far smoother than you could have done dry, but you'll be able to do an entire tabletop on just one sheet of sandpaper.
By some mineral oil, and keep your mouth shut when you do. You'll thank me for it.
key_insight: Mineral oil from the pharmacy aisle is the cheapest and most effective wet-sanding lubricant for woodworking, producing superior finishes while making sandpaper last far longer.
